#### Content:

Japan-Australia Summit Meeting (Summary)
October 26, 2025
Tweet
Japan-Australia Summit Meeting (1)
Japan-Australia Summit Meeting (2)
Japan-Australia Summit Meeting (3)
Japan-Australia Summit Meeting (4)
Japan-Australia Summit Meeting (5)
[Provisional Translation]
On October 26, commencing at 6:15 p.m. local time (7:15 p.m. on October 26, Japan time) for approximately 20 minutes, Ms. TAKAICHI Sanae, Prime Minister of Japan, who is visiting Malaysia to attend the ASEAN-related Summits’ meetings, held a Japan-Australia Summit Meeting with the Hon. Anthony Albanese, MP, Prime Minister of the Commonwealth of Australia. The overview of the meeting is as follows:
(1) At the outset, Prime Minister Albanese conveyed his congratulations on Prime Minister TAKAICHI’s appointment as Prime Minister. In response, Prime Minister TAKAICHI expressed her gratitude and stated that Japan looks forward to strengthening the strategic cooperative relationship between Japan and Australia. She also mentioned that Japan and Australia are likeminded countries that have the will and capability to play a leading role in realizing a “Free and Open Indo-Pacific" (FOIP).
(2) The two leaders exchanged views on the security environment and strategic challenges in the Indo-Pacific region, including the situations in East Asia, and concurred that they would further strengthen cooperation.
(3) The two leaders shared concerns about the impact of recent export control measures on critical minerals on supply chains and concurred that Japan and Australia would advance concrete cooperation in the field of economic security, including supply chains for critical minerals.
(4) In addition, the two leaders affirmed that Japan and Australia would promote multi-layered cooperation with U.S. and other likeminded countries, including Japan-US-Australia, Japan-Australia-India-U.S.(Quad), and Japan-Australia-New Zealand-ROK. Furthermore, they concurred that they would strengthen bilateral cooperation across a broad range of areas, including security, economic, and people-to-people exchanges to elevate the ‘Special Strategic Partnership’ between Japan and Australia to even greater heights, looking ahead to next year’s 50th anniversary of the signing of the Japan-Australia Basic Treaty of Friendship and Cooperation.

### Japan-Malaysia Summit Meeting (Summary)

- **Source:** Government of Japan
- **URL:** https://japan.kantei.go.jp/104/diplomatic/202510/26malaysia.html

Japan-Malaysia Summit Meeting (Summary)
October 26, 2025
Tweet
Japan-Malaysia Summit Meeting (1)
Japan-Malaysia Summit Meeting (2)
Japan-Malaysia Summit Meeting (3)
Japan-Malaysia Summit Meeting (4)
Japan-Malaysia Summit Meeting (5)
[Provisional Translation]
On October 26, from 5:50 p.m. local time (6:50 p.m. Japan time) and lasted approximately 20 minutes, Ms. TAKAICHI Sanae, Prime Minister of Japan, who is visiting Malaysia to attend the ASEAN-related Summit Meetings, held a meeting with H.E. Dato’ Seri Anwar bin Ibrahim, Prime Minister of Malaysia. The overview is as follows.
(1) At the outset, Prime Minister Anwar welcomed Prime Minister TAKAICHI’s participation in the ASEAN-related Summit Meetings and expressed his intention to work closely with Prime Minister TAKAICHI for the further development of the friendly relations between the two countries. In response, Prime Minister TAKAICHI expressed her respect for Malaysia’s efforts as the ASEAN Chair and stated her wish to further strengthen cooperation in various fields with Malaysia, which is an important comprehensive and strategic partner for the realization of a Free and Open Indo-Pacific (FOIP).
(2) Regarding cooperation in the field of security, the two leaders welcomed the hand-over of unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) and rescue boats from Japan to Malaysia through Japan’s Official Security Assistance (OSA) as well as the new agreement for the provision of a diving support vessel and other equipment. Prime Minister TAKAICHI stated that through such efforts she wished to contribute to regional peace and stability, to which Prime Minister Anwar expressed his appreciation for Japan’s cooperation.
In the economic field, the two leaders highly appreciated the progress in bilateral decarbonization cooperation between the two countries, such as Carbon dioxide Capture and Storage (CCS), ammonia-fired power generation and nuclear energy, and concurred to accelerate discussions toward the early signing of the Joint Crediting Mechanism (JCM) and to strengthen the initiatives of the Asia Zero-Emission Community (AZEC). They also confirmed that they will further enhance cooperation in economic security such as stable LNG supply, rare earths, and AI.
(3) The two leaders exchanged views on various regional and international issues, including the situation in the South China Sea, the situation in Myanmar, and developments in the Middle East.
Prime Minister TAKAICHI expressed her respect for Prime Minister Anwar’s efforts in the ceasefire agreement between the Kingdom of Cambodia and the Kingdom of Thailand, and conveyed Japan’s decision to provide Malaysia with monitoring equipment and supporting ceasefire agreement.

#### Content:

The 3rd Asia Zero-Emission Community (AZEC) Leaders Meeting (Summary)
October 26, 2025
Tweet
The 3rd Asia Zero-Emission Community (AZEC) Leaders Meeting (1)
The 3rd Asia Zero-Emission Community (AZEC) Leaders Meeting (2)
The 3rd Asia Zero-Emission Community (AZEC) Leaders Meeting (3)
The 3rd Asia Zero-Emission Community (AZEC) Leaders Meeting (4)
[Provisional Translation]
On October 26, commencing at 17:34 local time (18:34 JST) for approximately 10 min, Ms. TAKAICHI Sanae, the Prime Minister of Japan chaired the 3rd Asia Zero Emission Community (AZEC) Leaders Meeting with Dato' Seri Anwar bin Ibrahim, the Prime Minister of Malaysia. The summary of the meeting is as follows:
(1) Prime Minister Anwar, the co-chair of the meeting and this year’s ASEAN chair, showcased Malaysia and other ASEAN countries’ initiatives that align with the ideas of AZEC, and expressed his expectations for progress in tangible cooperation projects under AZEC.
(2) Prime Minister TAKAICHI acknowledged that approximately 120 cooperation projects have been developed between Japan and the AZEC partner countries for one year since the 2nd AZEC Leaders Meeting. The Prime Minister also stated that the efforts, including the development of rules to promote activities that contribute to decarbonization in Asia, sectoral initiatives and individual projects involving both the public and private sectors, have been showing progress.
(3) During the meeting, the Government of Japan reported on the outcomes of the 3rd AZEC Ministerial Meeting held on October 17, 2025.
(4) At this meeting, the leaders of the AZEC partner countries adopted the AZEC Leaders’ Joint Statement and its attachment, “The Progress Made in 2024-2025 Following the Action Plan for Next Decade”. These documents reaffirmed the importance of AZEC principles and the need to accelerate the actions in this critical decade. The leaders of the AZEC partner countries also shared the progress made over the past year based on the "Action Plan for the Next Decade" adopted last year.
(Note) AZEC partner countries (in alphabetical order):
Australia, Brunei Darussalam, Cambodia, Indonesia, Japan, Lao PDR, Malaysia, the Philippines, Singapore, Thailand, and Viet Nam

### The 28th ASEAN Japan Summit (Summary)

The 28th ASEAN Japan Summit (Summary)
October 26, 2025
Tweet

The 28th ASEAN Japan Summit (17)
The 28th ASEAN Japan Summit (18)
The 28th ASEAN Japan Summit (19)
The 28th ASEAN Japan Summit (20)
[Provisional Translation]
On October 26, commencing at 4:10 p.m. local time (5:10 pm, JST) for approximately 65 minutes, the 28th ASEAN-Japan Summit was held in Kuala Lumpur, the capital of Malaysia. Ms. TAKAICHI Sanae, Prime Minister of Japan, attended the meeting. The overview of the meeting is as follows.
1. ASEAN-Japan cooperation
(1) The meeting began with an opening remark by Dato' Seri Anwar bin Ibrahim, the Prime Minister of Malaysia and chairman of the meeting. At the beginning of her remarks, Prime Minister Takaichi expressed her deep condolences to the passing of Her Majesty Queen Sirikit, The Queen Mother of Thailand. Then Prime Minister Takaichi expressed her appreciation for the warm welcome and efforts of Prime Minister Anwar and the Malaysian government as the ASEAN Chair, as well as for the cooperation of Prime Minister Wong of Singapore as the Country Coordinator for ASEAN-Japan. She then stated as follows:
i. I would like to congratulate Prime Minister Gusmao on Timor Leste’s official accession to ASEAN.
ii. It is an honor for me to meet with ASEAN leaders on my first foreign visit as Prime Minister. Japan and ASEAN have built a relationship as "Trusted Partners" with "heart to heart" connections through long-standing cooperation in a wide range of areas.
iii. Japan advocates for a "Free and Open Indo-Pacific (FOIP)" and consistently supports the " ASEAN Outlook on the Indo-Pacific (AOIP)," which shares fundamental principles with FOIP. It is very gratifying to adopt a joint statement with ASEAN leaders today (October 26) which affirmed the synergy between FOIP and AOIP and confirms our will to promote further cooperation between Japan and ASEAN.
iv. Next year marks the 10th anniversary since then-Prime Minister Abe launched FOIP in 2016. We will use this opportunity to reposition FOIP as a pillar of Japanese diplomacy and evolve it in line with changes in the times. We would like to further strengthen our collaboration with AOIP to make Japan and ASEAN stronger and more prosperous.
(2) Prime Minister Takaichi then shared with the ASEAN leaders the progress Japan and ASEAN have made in line with the three pillars outlined in the 2023 Joint Vision Statement for the 50th Year of ASEAN-Japan Friendship and Cooperation.
i.
“Partners for Peace and Stability”
- Japan has been supporting the strengthening of ASEAN's maritime security through Official Development Assistance (ODA). Japan has initiated cooperation with some countries through Official Security Assistance (OSA) and will expand it to other ASEAN countries and vigorously promote cooperation in areas such as disaster response, in addition to maritime security.
- We will also strengthen our efforts through initiatives such as the ASEAN-Japan Cybersecurity Policy Meeting and the ASEAN-Japan Cybersecurity Capacity Building Centre (AJCCBC) , along with cooperation to combat transnational crimes, including organized fraud.
ii.
“Partners for Co-creation of Economy and Society of the Future”
- We will strengthen cooperation in many areas such as digitalization including AI, green initiatives including decarbonization, sustainable agriculture, forestry, and fisheries, supply chain resilience including the automotive sector, connectivity, and regional financial cooperation.
- Japan proposes the launch of Japan-ASEAN Co-creation Initiative for Artificial Intelligence (AI). We aim to jointly build a safe, secure, and trustworthy AI ecosystem through model development, human resource development, and co-creation of solutions.
- We will strengthen international joint research and researcher exchanges in cutting-edge fields such as AI, quantum technology, and semiconductors.
- To respond to increasingly frequent and severe disasters and provide humanitarian assistance, Japan will continue to support the activities and human resource development of the ASEAN Coordinating Centre for Humanitarian Assistance on Disaster Management (AHA Centre). Japan is also dispatching experts to support the establishment and capacity building of the ASEAN Centre for Public Health Emergencies and Emerging Diseases (ACPHEED).
iii.
“Heart-to-Heart Partners across Generation”
- We will steadily and effectively implement the WA Project ("WA Project" and its successor, “Partnership to Co-create a Future with the Next Generation: WA Project 2.0,)” consisting of “NIHONGO Partners”(assistant Japanese language teachers), with approximately 3,000 participants, as well as intellectual and cultural exchanges.
- There are active youth exchanges through programs such as JENESYS and the Ship for Southeast Asian and Japanese Youth Program, which will celebrate its 50th voyage next fiscal year. Student exchanges facilitated by associations of former international students in Japan, remain active.
- The Attachment Program, which is funded by the Japan-ASEAN Integration Fund (JAIF), began accepting participants from Timor-Leste this year. This program dispatches young civil servants to the ASEAN Secretariat. Additionally, a new initiative has commenced to support program graduates in studying at the National Graduate Institute for Policy Studies (GRIPS) in Japan.
- Working closely with ASEAN Member States, we will further develop existing ASEAN-Japan cooperation and advance initiatives to make Japan and ASEAN stronger and more prosperous together. Together with ASEAN, we are committed to safeguarding peace, stability, and prosperity in the Indo-Pacific region.
(3) The ASEAN Member States successively expressed congratulations on Prime Minister Takaichi's appointment and welcomed her attendance at the ASEAN-Japan Summit.
They also praised Japan's cooperation in various fields. They expressed particular appreciation for Japan's support of the ASEAN Outlook on the Indo-Pacific (AOIP) and welcomed the adoption of the
"Joint Statement of the 28th ASEAN-Japan Summit on Further Promotion and Implementation of the AOIP."
Furthermore, they expressed high expectations for strengthening cooperation with Japan in new areas, such as digital and green technologies, and for promoting economic partnerships through the ASEAN-Japan Comprehensive Economic Partnership (AJCEP), among others. Many countries also welcomed the launch of the Japan-ASEAN AI Co-creation Initiative proposed by Prime Minister Takaichi.
Additionally, they expressed high regard and expectations for Japan's cooperation in cultural and youth exchanges, education, and science and technology. This includes the WA Project, JENESYS, the Ship for Southeast Asian Youth Program.
(4) In response to the ASEAN leaders' remarks, Prime Minister Takaichi proposed forming a working group to strengthen Japan-ASEAN economic relations and discuss various measures, considering ASEAN's interest in digital and green issues.
2. Regional and International Issues
(1) Concerns about the situations in the South China Sea, the importance of peaceful resolution of disputes in accordance with the United Nations Convention on the Law of the Sea (UNCLOS), the importance of denuclearization of North Korea in accordance with United Nations Security Council resolutions and resolution of the abductions issue, and appreciation for Japan's humanitarian assistance for Myanmar were mentioned in the meeting.
(2) In response, Prime Minister Takaichi made the following remarks:
i. General Principles
The free and open international order based on the rule of law is currently facing challenges, and the security environment in the Indo-Pacific region is becoming increasingly severe. Attempts to unilaterally change the status quo by force or coercion must not be tolerated anywhere in the world.
ii. The East China Sea, the South China Sea, Taiwan
In the East China Sea, activities that infringe upon Japan's sovereignty, as well as provocative military activities, continue and are intensifying. In the South China Sea, militarization and coercive activities continue and intensify, causing serious concern. Any unjust maritime claims and activities that are not based on the UNCLOS cannot be accepted. Japan consistently supports the rule of law at sea and reaffirms the importance of resolving disputes peacefully based on international law. Peace and stability across the Taiwan Strait are important issues, which directly impact regional security.
iii. North Korea
North Korea conducted the ballistic missile launch on October 22. North Korea has steadily enhanced its nuclear and missile capabilities, funded by its cryptocurrency thefts, and gained combat experience through military cooperation with Russia. Japan is seriously concerned about these actions that heighten regional tensions. The complete denuclearization of North Korea in accordance with UN Security Council resolutions is essential. Japan reiterates its call for the full implementation of these resolutions and for coordinated action. Furthermore, the immediate resolution of the abductions issue by North Korea is an urgent matter. Japan calls for the continued understanding and cooperation from the ASEAN countries toward the immediate resolution of the abductions issue.
iv. Situation in Myanmar
Japan is deeply concerned about the situation in Myanmar. We strongly urge the Myanmar military to immediately stop the violence and start political dialogue.
v. Middle East Situation
Peace and stability in the Middle East are of the utmost importance to Japan. We strongly urge all parties to faithfully and steadily implement the agreement for the ceasefire and the release of hostages. Japan will play an active role in improving the humanitarian situation, supporting early recovery and reconstruction, and realizing a two-state solution, working together with ASEAN including through the Conference on Cooperation among East Asian countries for Palestinian Development (CEAPAD).
vi. Russia's aggression against Ukraine
Russia’s aggression against Ukraine constitutes a grave violation of international law. It is crucial that all nations, united as a global community, engage in diplomatic efforts to achieve a just and lasting peace in Ukraine.
3. Closing Remarks
In closing, Prime Minister Takaichi expressed gratitude once again for the warm welcome extended by the ASEAN leaders. She made the following remarks:
i. Hearing the enthusiastic statements from ASEAN countries about strengthening cooperation with Japan has renewed our commitment to strengthening our relationship.
ii. Japan will continue to support ASEAN's efforts to promote integration based on the ASEAN Community Vision 2045 and to mainstream the ASEAN Outlook on the Indo-Pacific (AOIP).
iii. Furthermore, she expressed her gratitude to the ASEAN Member States and the Secretariat for their participation in Expo 2025 Osaka, Kansai, which concluded earlier this month. The Expo was a great success and deepened our friendship with each country.
iv. Going forward, we will continue to work closely with leaders of ASEAN countries to strengthen both bilateral relations and ASEAN-Japan relations. As a "Trusted Partner," we aim to pass on a "Free and Open Indo-Pacific" and a peaceful, stable, and prosperous future to the next generation.

---

Japan-Philippines Summit Meeting (Summary)
October 26, 2025
Tweet
Japan-Philippines Summit Meeting (1)
Japan-Philippines Summit Meeting (2)
Japan-Philippines Summit Meeting (3)
Japan-Philippines Summit Meeting (4)
Japan-Philippines Summit Meeting (5)
[Provisional Translation]
On October 26, commencing at 13:09 local time (14:09 JST) for approximately 20 min, Ms. TAKAICHI Sanae, Prime Minister of Japan, who is visiting Malaysia to attend the ASEAN-related Summit meetings, held a Japan-Philippines Summit Meeting with H.E. Ferdinand R. Marcos, Jr., President of the Republic of the Philippines. The overview of the meeting is as follows.
(1) At the outset, Prime Minister Takaichi expressed her pleasure to hold a meeting with President Marcos soon after assuming office. She stated her desire to further strengthen relations with the Philippines, as a strategic partner, toward realizing a Free and Open Indo-Pacific (FOIP). President Marcos congratulations to Prime Minister Takaichi on her appointment and stated his desire to strengthen relations between the two countries on the occasion of the 70th anniversary of the normalization of diplomatic relations.
(2) Regarding security cooperation, both leaders welcomed that Japan and the Philippines reached an agreement in principle on an Acquisition and Cross-Servicing Agreement (ACSA). They also welcomed the entry into force of the Japan-Philippines Reciprocal Access Agreement (RAA) in September this year, its first application this month to the joint exercise and the transport of support goods following the Cebu earthquake, and the progress of cooperation through the Official Security Assistance (OSA). Both leaders concurred on continuing working to strengthen the foundation for security cooperation that contributes to the peace and stability of the Philippines and the region.
(3) Regarding the economic sector, Prime Minister Takaichi conveyed Japan's desire to leverage its strengths to promote the resilience of the Philippine's economy and infrastructure. She also stated Japan's intention to contribute to the Philippine’s food security through the provision of equipment for post-harvest rice processing. President Marcos expressed his gratitude for Japan’s cooperation through ODA and stated his desire to further strengthen cooperation not only in ODA but also across a wide range of fields.
Furthermore, Prime Minister Takaichi mentioned the signing of the MOC on comprehensive energy cooperation between Japan and the Philippines at the AZEC Ministerial Meeting in this October. Prime Minister Takaichi conveyed Japan’s intention to advance civil nuclear cooperation and to cooperate toward the success of AZEC next year.
(4) Next year marks the 70th anniversary of the normalization of diplomatic relations and the Philippines will assure the ASEAN Chairmanship. Taking this into account, both leaders concurred on continuing close coordination in addressing various international challenges, including the situation in the South China Sea, responses to North Korea encompassing nuclear and missile issues and the abduction issue, the situations in Myanmar, and the situations in the Cambodia-Thailand border areas.

---

### Contributed Article "Making Japan and ASEAN stronger and more prosperous together" by Prime Minister TAKAICHI Sanae to the Malaysian newspapers The New Straits Times, The Berita Harian, and The Sin Chew Daily

- **Source:** Government of Japan
- **URL:** https://japan.kantei.go.jp/104/statement/202510/26message_00001.html
- **Published Date:** 2025-10-26
- **Scraped Date:** 2026-03-23
- **Word Count:** 909
- **Unique ID:** `64f4ce22f3f8ecbd`

#### Content:

Contributed Article "Making Japan and ASEAN stronger and more prosperous together" by Prime Minister TAKAICHI Sanae to the Malaysian newspapers The New Straits Times, The Berita Harian, and The Sin Chew Daily
October 26, 2025
Tweet
[Provisional translation]
I extend my heartfelt congratulations on Malaysia’s hosting of the ASEAN Summit and Related Meetings. It is my great pleasure to visit Malaysia as my first overseas destination since assuming the office of Prime Minister and to participate in this prestigious event.
This year marks several historic milestones, including the 10th anniversary of the establishment of the ASEAN Community, the adoption of the ASEAN Community Vision 2045, and the 20th anniversary of the establishment of the East Asia Summit (EAS). As the heart of the Indo-Pacific region and a global center of growth, ASEAN is becoming increasingly important. I commend Malaysia for promoting regional prosperity through vibrant trade and investment, and for its exceptional leadership as the ASEAN Chair this year. Japan fully supports ASEAN’s resolve and Malaysia’s efforts as the ASEAN Chair to realize a “resilient, innovative, dynamic, and people-centered ASEAN Community.”
Japan and ASEAN have been building a relationship as “Trusted Partners” with “heart-to-heart” ties for over half a century. In January 2013, shortly after his administration took office, then-Prime Minister Shinzo Abe also visited Southeast Asia as his first overseas destination, and outlined Japan’s basic foreign policy toward ASEAN. The Joint Vision Statement on the 50th Year of ASEAN-Japan Friendship and Cooperation, which was adopted in December 2023, outlined the three pillars of our partnership: Peace and Stability, Co-creation of the Economy and Society of the Future, and Heart-to-Heart Partners across Generations. Building on these friendly ties, I will also do my utmost to ensure that Japan and ASEAN become stronger and more prosperous together.
Japan and ASEAN have also undertaken various initiatives involving people-to-people exchanges. For example, we have been steadily and effectively implementing the “WA Project,” which has two main focuses: a “Two-way Intellectual and Cultural Exchange” and “NIHONGO Partners.” The latter entails dispatching assistant Japanese language teachers to host countries in the Asian region and has a track record of approximately 3,000 participants. Furthermore, we have been actively conducting youth exchanges through programs like “The Ship for Southeast Asian and Japanese Youth Program (SSEAYP),” which celebrated its 50th anniversary last year, and JENESYS (the Japan-East Asia Network of Exchange for Students and Youths), as well as student exchanges facilitated by ASCOJA (the ASEAN Council of Japan Alumni) and its supporting organization, ASJA (Asia Japan Alumni) International. Through these initiatives, we will deepen trust and mutual understanding among the next generation of leaders.
Based on this trust and mutual understanding, strategic and proactive investment in new fields such as digital and green technologies is essential for Japan and ASEAN to co-create the economy and society of the future and grow together.
In the digital field, AI has great potential to significantly transform and develop the economy and society. Therefore, Japan will promote “safe, secure, and trustworthy AI,” advance the establishment of international AI governance, and promote innovation utilizing AI. Science and technology, which form the basis of innovation, are also important. ASEAN is an important partner in achieving the goal I have set of becoming a “new nation of technology.” We will strengthen international joint research and researcher exchanges in fields involving frontier technologies such as AI, quantum technology, and semiconductors in flexible and multilayered ways.
In addition, regarding the green sector, I will co-host the Asia Zero Emission Community (AZEC) Leaders’ Meeting with Prime Minister Anwar of Malaysia during my visit. Using various technologies, we will advance tangible cooperation with ASEAN countries, in line with their respective transition pathways, to simultaneously achieve decarbonization, economic growth, and energy security.
Furthermore, to respond to increasingly severe disasters and provide humanitarian assistance, Japan will continue supporting the activities and human resource development of the ASEAN Coordinating Centre for Humanitarian Assistance on Disaster Management (AHA Centre). Japanese experts are also actively contributing to the establishment of the ASEAN Centre for Public Health Emergencies and Emerging Diseases (ACPHEED) and capacity-building support.
While moving forward with such exchanges and cooperation, we must also confront the reality that the free and open international order based on the rule of law faces unprecedented challenges in various parts of the world. Today, the partnership between Japan and ASEAN for peace and stability is of utmost importance. Whether in Asia, Europe, or anywhere in the world, unilateral attempts to change the status quo by force must not be tolerated.
Japan’s Free and Open Indo-Pacific (FOIP) and the ASEAN Outlook on the Indo-Pacific (AOIP) share fundamental principles, such as openness, transparency, inclusiveness, and respect for the rule of law. Furthermore, we will adopt a joint statement at this ASEAN-Japan Summit to promote synergies and further cooperation between FOIP and AOIP for peace, stability, and prosperity in the Indo-Pacific region.
Finally, I would like to express my renewed gratitude to Malaysia and the other ASEAN Member States, and to the ASEAN Secretariat, for their  participation and cooperation in Expo 2025 Osaka, Kansai, Japan, which was successfully concluded recently. I would like to add that many Japanese citizens visited the Malaysia Pavilion, themed “Weaving a Future in Harmony,” experienced its diverse and rich culture, and found Malaysia to be an even more familiar and attractive country for Japan.
As a “Trusted Partner,” Japan remains committed to further strengthening its broad cooperation with Malaysia and other ASEAN member states.

---

### Press Conference by Prime Minister TAKAICHI Sanae Regarding the Japan-U.S. Summit Telephone Talks

- **Source:** Government of Japan
- **URL:** https://japan.kantei.go.jp/104/statement/202510/25kaiken_00002.html
- **Published Date:** 2025-10-25
- **Scraped Date:** 2026-03-23
- **Word Count:** 302
- **Unique ID:** `a8d6cd3bb0ffe174`

#### Content:

Press Conference by Prime Minister TAKAICHI Sanae Regarding the Japan-U.S. Summit Telephone Talks
October 25, 2025
Tweet
[Provisional translation]
(On Prime Minister Takaichi's phone call with U.S. President Donald Trump)
I just finished a phone call with President Trump. First of all, he warmly congratulated me on my appointment as Prime Minister, and I was very grateful for his kind words. I conveyed to him that strengthening the Japan–U.S. Alliance is the top priority for my administration’s foreign and security policy. We confirmed our shared commitment to further elevating the Alliance to new heights. I also expressed my respect for President Trump’s leadership regarding the recent situation in the Middle East. I conveyed that Japan is an indispensable partner for the United States, both in its strategy toward China and in its Indo-Pacific strategy, and I conveyed my will to continue to advance the vision of a Free and Open Indo-Pacific (FOIP) together.
In addition, I conveyed my appreciation to President Trump for his support to date on the abduction issue and asked for his continued understanding and cooperation going forward.
Lastly, I mentioned that I very much look forward to welcoming the President in Tokyo in the coming days.
(On whether President Trump was making the call from Air Force One)
Yes.
(On Prime Minister Takaichi's impression of U.S. President Donald Trump after directly talking to him)
My impression of President Trump is that he is a very cheerful and engaging person. He knew quite a bit about myself already and even shared with me some fond memories of the late Prime Minister Abe. He said he knew I was one of the politicians Prime Minister Abe cared deeply about. I, in turn, also expressed my gratitude for the kindness and friendship he has shown to Mrs. Akie Abe this year.

---

### Japan-U.S. Summit Telephone Talk (Summary)

- **Source:** Government of Japan
- **URL:** https://japan.kantei.go.jp/104/diplomatic/202510/25usa_00001.html
- **Published Date:** 2025-10-25
- **Scraped Date:** 2026-03-23
- **Word Count:** 230
- **Unique ID:** `e9aded9fa43d1105`

#### Content:

Japan-U.S. Summit Telephone Talk (Summary)
October 25, 2025
Tweet
[Provisional Translation]
On October 25, commencing at 9:30 PM, local time, (10:30 PM, Japan Standard Time) for approximately 10 minutes, Ms. TAKAICHI Sanae, Prime Minister of Japan, who is visiting Kuala Lumpur, Malaysia to attend the ASEAN-related Summit Meetings, held a summit telephone talk with the Honorable Donald J. Trump, President of the United States of America. The overview is as follows.
(1) At the outset, President Trump offered his congratulations to Prime Minister Takaichi on her assumption of office. Prime Minister Takaichi stated that strengthening the Japan-U.S. Alliance is a top priority of Japan’s foreign and security policy, and the two leaders confirmed that they would cooperate to continue elevating the Japan-U.S. Alliance to new heights.
(2) Prime Minister Takaichi stated that Japan is an indispensable partner for the U.S. in the Indo-Pacific, and expressed her intention to work together toward realizing a free and open Indo-Pacific (FOIP). Prime Minister Takaichi also expressed her respect towards President Trump’s leadership in achieving the agreement in the Middle East.
(3) Prime Minister Takaichi also asked for continued understanding and cooperation of the U.S. for the immediate resolution of the abductions issue.
(4) Prime Minister Takaichi stated that she looks forward to welcoming President Trump to Japan, and President Trump also stated that he looks forward to meeting Prime Minister Takaichi in person.

### Press Conference by Prime Minister Takaichi regarding Her Attendance at the Upcoming ASEAN-related Summit Meetings

- **Source:** Government of Japan
- **URL:** https://japan.kantei.go.jp/104/statement/202510/25kaiken_00007.html
- **Published Date:** 2025-10-25
- **Scraped Date:** 2026-03-23
- **Word Count:** 276
- **Unique ID:** `c020e68b743aa0ce`

#### Content:

Press Conference by Prime Minister Takaichi regarding Her Attendance at the Upcoming ASEAN-related Summit Meetings
October 25, 2025
Tweet
[Provisional translation]
(On Prime Minister Takaichi's attendance at the ASEAN (the Association of Southeast Asian Nations) related Summit Meetings as the first overseas trip, and on the results the Prime Minister intends to achieve through meetings with leaders of ASEAN and other participating leaders)
I will be traveling to Malaysia to attend the series of ASEAN-related Summit Meetings. ASEAN is situated at the crossroads of the Indian and Pacific Oceans. It’s also one of the world’s key growth centers, and a core pillar of Japan’s “Free and Open Indo-Pacific” (FOIP) initiative. I intend to use this opportunity to deepen mutual trust with the leaders of ASEAN and to achieve significant results.
Following that, there will also be the AZEC Leaders’ Meeting, which Japan and Malaysia will co-chair.
Since I will be in Malaysia, I also plan to hold a number of bilateral meetings with leaders — not only with the host country, Malaysia, but also with the leaders of the Philippines and Australia, as I mentioned in my policy speech yesterday. Through these talks, I aim to contribute to the peace and prosperity of the region, which is directly tied to protecting Japan’s national interests.
Including my remote participation in last night’s conference held in London (Virtual Leaders Meeting of the Coalition of the Willing on Ukraine), this marks the second major step in my diplomatic schedule. Japan’s diplomacy will continue to flourish at the heart of the international community.
(On whether the reported phone call with U.S. President Donald Trump has taken place)
No, not yet.

### Message of Congratulations from Prime Minister TAKAICHI on the Occasion of the 12th Mt. Fuji Dialogue

- **Source:** Government of Japan
- **URL:** https://japan.kantei.go.jp/104/statement/202510/25message.html
- **Published Date:** 2025-10-25
- **Scraped Date:** 2026-03-23
- **Word Count:** 414
- **Unique ID:** `0472819a270dac40`

#### Content:

Message of Congratulations from Prime Minister TAKAICHI on the Occasion of the 12th Mt. Fuji Dialogue
October 25, 2025
Tweet
[Provisional translation]
On October 25, Prime Minister TAKAICHI Sanae delivered the following message of congratulations on the occasion of the 12th Mt. Fuji Dialogue, which was read out on her behalf by Cabinet Secretary for Public Affairs KOBAYASHI.
“I would like to extend my heartfelt congratulations on the occasion of the 12th Mt. Fuji Dialogue.
Although I was unable to attend the meeting today, I would like to express my deep respect to the Japan Center for Economic Research, the Japan Institute of International Affairs, and all those involved for their tremendous efforts in making this dialogue possible.
The Mt. Fuji Dialogue has long served as a premier dialogue of eminent persons from Japan and the United States, fostering intellectual exchange between our two nations and making significant contributions to the strengthening of the Japan-U.S. Alliance.
As confrontations and divisions deepen across the world and the security environment we face grows increasingly severe, it is essential to further strengthen the Japan-U.S. Alliance, which serves as the cornerstone of regional peace and prosperity.
Amid such circumstances, I will proactively engage in a series of important diplomatic events scheduled soon after assuming the office of Prime Minister. To begin with, I will attend the ASEAN-related summit meetings starting today, followed by the visit to Japan by President Trump next week. Subsequently, the APEC Economic Leaders’ Meeting will also take place.
In particular, President Trump’s visit to Japan will serve as an opportunity to deepen the relationship of trust between President Trump and me, and to elevate Japan-U.S. relations to new heights. The Japan-U.S. Alliance is the cornerstone of Japan’s foreign and security policy. Beyond bilateral issues, I will work closely with President Trump through candid exchanges of views on a wide range of challenges that Japan and the United States face—from those in the Indo-Pacific region to the situations in the Middle East, Europe, and Ukraine. To protect Japan’s national interests, I am determined to restore Japan’s diplomacy that flourishes on the world's center stage.
In closing, I sincerely hope that the Mt. Fuji Dialogue will continue to develop as a dialogue that brings together wisdom across the public, private, and academic sectors, and that it will further flourish as an intellectual dialogue as grand as Mt. Fuji itself, with a beautiful and far-reaching base spreading outward.
October 25, 2025
TAKAICHI Sanae
Prime Minister of Japan”

### Policy Speech by Prime Minister TAKAICHI Sanae to the 219th Session of the Diet

- **Source:** Government of Japan
- **URL:** https://japan.kantei.go.jp/104/statement/202510/24shoshinhyomei.html
- **Published Date:** 2025-10-24
- **Scraped Date:** 2026-03-23
- **Word Count:** 4728
- **Unique ID:** `6ed3f18eb7086c11`

#### Content:

Policy Speech by Prime Minister TAKAICHI Sanae to the 219th Session of the Diet
October 24, 2025
Tweet
[Provisional translation]
Introduction
Basic policy on economic and fiscal initiatives
Measures to address rising prices
Achieving robust economic growth through bold strategic investments that enhance resilience against potential crises
Food security
Energy security
Measures to enhance national resilience during the Reiwa era
Health and medical security
Preserving our regional areas and our ways of life
Diplomacy and security
Constitutional reform, revision of the Imperial Household Law, and commemorating the 100th anniversary of the start of the Showa era
Conclusion
1. Introduction
Bolstered by a strong and unwavering belief in the latent power and vitality of Japan and the Japanese people, I stand before you today as I take on the duty of forging a path for Japan's future.
I will strive to build a robust economy, turning people's unease and apprehension over their current lives and the future into hope and foster a Japan that is stronger and more prosperous. I will deal with the challenges facing the international community as I embark on restoring Japanese diplomacy that flourishes on the world's center stage. I will do these resolutely for the sake of Japan and the Japanese people, filled with the determination to press ahead tenaciously under any circumstances.
We will be powerless to press forward with dynamic economic policies or potent diplomatic and security policies if we fail to have stability in politics. Guided by this thought, I have established a coalition government between the Liberal Democratic Party and the Japan Innovation Party under a wide-ranging policy agreement that aims at the revival of Japan.
Moreover, we will make politics stable for the benefit of both the nation and the Japanese people. We will accept policy proposals from other parties as long as they are not inconsistent with the Basic Policy of this administration and demonstrate flexibility and sincerity when discussing them. We will make all-out efforts to undertake reforms that restore the public's trust in politics.
We will persevere to the very end, whatever the circumstances, if it is for Japan and the Japanese people. This is the unshakable policy of the Takaichi Cabinet.
2. Basic policy on economic and fiscal initiatives
Fostering a robust economy will be absolutely essential, no matter what we seek to implement. I will now talk about the basic policy governing the economic and fiscal initiatives we will pursue to bring about such an economy.
My cabinet regards as fundamental the principle that the economy is what enables fiscal spending. In order to build a robust economy, we will strategically employ fiscal policies as we endeavor to raise incomes, transform people's mindsets regarding consumption, and boost tax revenues without raising tax rates as business earnings increase, all in keeping with our approach of responsible and proactive public finances. By realizing this economic virtuous cycle, we will enable the public to feel the fruits of economic recovery in a tangible manner, thereby changing worry into hope.
Following this path, we will curb the rate of growth of Japan's outstanding debt balance so as not to exceed the rate of economic growth and lower Japan's ratio of outstanding government debt to GDP. This will bring about the sustainability of public finances and ensure trust from the markets.
3. Measures to address rising prices
My cabinet will mount responses to the rising prices facing the Japanese people, as a matter of the highest priority. We will swiftly and without fail bring a sense of security to people in their daily lives.
While it is essential to have wage increases that surpass the rise in prices, leaving the matter entirely up to businesses only results in making it difficult for business operators to run their companies. The role of government lies in preparing an environment in which wage increases can be undertaken in a sustained manner.
That said, it will take a certain amount of time until sustained increases in real wages become firmly rooted in society. It is also a matter of urgency to attend to business operators impacted by the U.S. tariff measures and provide assistance to medical institutions and nursing care facilities where operational difficulties are becoming increasingly severe.
I have already directed my cabinet to begin formulating economic measures. While repeatedly engaging in sincere discussions with members of opposition parties to build up consensus, we will compile measures rapidly and submit the necessary supplementary budget to the Diet. Let us bring together the insights of both the ruling and opposition parties so that we emerge with economic measures and a supplementary budget that will safeguard people's daily lives and livelihoods.
We will not implement the stipend pledged by the Liberal Democratic Party during this summer's House of Councillors election campaign because it failed to gain the understanding of the public. We will instead take well-crafted responses to the concerns of the public regarding rising prices, meticulously addressing each one of them.
First of all, we will endeavor to pass a bill during the current Diet session that abolishes the so-called provisional tax rate on gasoline, through discussions among the various political parties. We also aim for the early abolition of the provisional tax rate on the delivery of diesel oil. While ensuring stable revenue for both the national and local governments, which will become necessary as we abolish these provisional tax rates, we will contend with the drop in prices by making use of subsidies until we have succeeded in abolishing these provisional tax rates.
Dealing with medical institutions and nursing care facilities struggling as they operate in the red is a matter of great urgency if we are to safeguard people's lives and ensure that people are able to receive necessary services without anxiety. Going forward, we will appropriately reflect the increases in both wages and prices within our reimbursements for medical care and nursing care services provided. However, instead of waiting for the timing of regular revisions to these reimbursements, we will set up subsidies designed to support managerial reforms and improve employee treatment, precipitating the desired benefits ahead of schedule.
In addition, we will conduct an appropriate review of unit prices for contracted work offered from the national and local governments to the private sector, based on price increases and other relevant factors.
We will protect small- and medium-sized enterprises (SMEs) and small-scale business operators from rising costs. We will strongly support wage increases and capital investment through means such as assistance to boost productivity, efforts to cultivate an environment facilitating business succession and mergers and acquisitions, and further efforts to ensure proper business transactions.
We will expand our provision of grants to local regions for prioritized assistance, aimed at assisting local governments. We will create a menu of recommended measures that provide support for consumers impacted by high prices, SMEs and small-scale business operators that are unable to make use of the tax scheme promoting wage increases, and agriculture, forestry, and fisheries businesses. We will deliver well-targeted support suited to the actual situation found in each local area, and alongside this, we will provide assistance for electricity and gas bills during the winter months when the cold is most severe.
With regard to the disincentives for second earners to earn more than a certain amount of income annually, known as the “barrier of 1.03 million yen,” we will raise the income limit [qualifying for dependent deductions] to 1.60 million yen during the year-end adjustment for 2025. We will also advance our discussions in a sincere manner on a tax measure to further raise the basic deduction in a way that is linked to changes in prices.
As for making high school tuition and school lunches effectively free, we will build upon the multiple discussions held thus far transcending party and factional lines and advance our discussions on how to design the relevant systems, secure stable funding for these initiatives, and implement both of them from next April. We will take this opportunity to go beyond only providing financial support and press forward with a review of what high school education in Japan should look like.
We must also reduce the burden borne by middle- and low-income earners, who struggle to shoulder taxes and social insurance premiums, and ensure that people's net incomes increase as their earnings rise. We will promptly begin designing a system for refundable tax credits.
To respond to the U.S. tariff measures, we will prepare a menu of support options, including cash-flow measures for SMEs, tailored to business operators' individual situations and needs, and take all possible measures to reduce the impact.
4. Achieving robust economic growth through bold strategic investments that enhance resilience against potential crises
It is vitally important for us to make Japan's "economic pie" larger over the medium to long term. We will aim to cultivate cutting-edge technologies that contribute to resolving the issues Japan faces, enabling the Japanese economy to realize vigorous growth. As a step towards that goal, we will launch the Council for Japan's Growth Strategy.
Strategic investments that enhance resilience against potential crises are the core focus of the Takaichi Cabinet's growth strategy. These are strategic investments that the public and private sector will implement preemptively, working hand in hand, to address various risks and social issues, including economic security, food security, energy security, health and medical security, and measures to enhance national resilience. If we succeed in providing products, services, and infrastructure that contribute to the resolution of commonly-shared global issues, that will lead to further economic growth for Japan. Transforming apprehension towards the future into hope, we will carve out new growth for the economy.
In strategic fields such as AI and semiconductors, shipbuilding, quantum technology, biotechnology, aviation and space, and cyber security, we will take comprehensive support measures from a multifaceted perspective that includes boldly promoting investment, supporting international expansion, cultivating human resources, promoting startups, conducting research and development, fostering collaboration between industry and academia, and international standardization of our products. By so doing, we will draw out proactive investment from both the public and the private sector.
Aiming to make Japan the world’s best country to develop and use AI, we will utilize data linkage and integration and other means to accelerate the research, development, and commercialization of AI and other new digital technologies. Beyond that, we will support the overseas expansion of industries related to digital technologies, including the content industry.
I offer my hearty congratulations to Dr. SAKAGUCHI Shimon and Dr. KITAGAWA Susumu on being awarded this year’s Nobel Prizes. The foundation of a robust economy is outstanding scientific and technological capabilities, together with human resources who are capable of promoting innovation.
We will push forward with our efforts to bolster public education and reform universities. We will at the same time provide strategic support that will help to advance science and engineering and contribute to fostering human resources, embracing the goal of being a new kind of technology-driven nation.
We will also need the power of finance to accelerate our growth strategy. Building upon the results of our efforts to shift capital from savings into investments as one part of our promotion of Japan as a leading asset management center, we will formulate a strategy that unleashes the latent potential of both the Japanese economy as a whole and local economies through financial mechanisms and implement such strategy through public-private cooperation.
By so doing, we will fortify Japan's supply structure and bring about an economy that invites trust from investors around the globe. This will give rise to a virtuous cycle in which capital from around the world flows into Japan.
5. Food security
From the perspective of revitalizing local communities and ensuring food security, it will be important to promote the agriculture, forestry and fishery industries. In the area of agriculture, we will secure a special budgetary allocation during the period for undertaking intensive measures to transform agricultural structures, which lasts five years. We will make use of world-class plant factories, land-based aquaculture, satellite data, AI analysis, and sensors, among other cutting-edge technologies, and promote exports, thereby fostering agriculture, forestry and fishery industries that turn a profit.
6. Energy security
The stable and inexpensive supply of energy is absolutely crucial in maintaining the daily lives of the Japanese people and nurturing our domestic industries while further strengthening the competitiveness of locations for attracting business investments. In particular, domestically-sourced energy, notably nuclear power and Perovskite solar cells, will be critical. Utilizing the Green Transformation (GX) budget, we will leverage decarbonized energy sources to the fullest possible extent while also pressing forward with comprehensive energy conservation and fuel switching by employing technologies such as photonics-electronics convergence technology. Such initiatives will be premised on gaining the understanding of the local communities and on giving due regard to environmental impacts. We will also strive for the early practical adoption in society of next-generation advanced reactors and fusion energy.
We will give concrete shape to these measures immediately. Let us make collective efforts across Japan to realize a robust economy.
7. Measures to enhance national resilience during the Reiwa era
Japan is one of the world's most disaster-prone countries. As such, proactive disaster prevention in anticipation of a potential massive earthquake such as an earthquake in the Nankai Trough or directly beneath the Tokyo metropolitan area and emergency response, recovery, and reconstruction in the wake of disasters should they actually occur are among the highest-priority issues for Japan as a nation.
Accordingly, we will accelerate preparations for the establishment in fiscal 2026 of a dedicated Disaster Management Agency, with a view to undertaking a fundamental reinforcement of our disaster management framework. Alongside this, we will carry out a comprehensive risk assessment through simulations conducted by both the national and local governments. We will also rigorously implement proactive disaster prevention and preventive maintenance in both structural and non-structural aspects, including through the use of digital technologies and satellite information, electromagnetic data, and drones, as well as through developing and maintaining disaster prevention infrastructure and aging infrastructure.
To counteract the increasing frequency and intensity of natural disasters, we will revise our systems and newly implement special flood warnings and joint forecasts and warnings for storm surges, made possible through improvements in forecasting technologies and other advances.
We will create a system that will serve as a backup to the crisis management functions of the Tokyo metropolitan area. From the perspective of decentralizing the functions of the capital and establishing a multi-centered economic zone, we will move quickly to consider the responsibilities and functions of the capital and secondary capital.
Without the reconstruction of Fukushima, there will be no reconstruction of Tohoku. And without the reconstruction of Tohoku, there will be no revival of Japan. Among other endeavors, we will work to rebuild the daily lives as well as the industries, occupations, and livelihoods of the disaster victims and promote the Fukushima Innovation Coast Framework.
Soon it will be two years since the Noto Peninsula Earthquake struck. And, a full year has already passed since the heavy rains battered Oku-Noto, which was in the midst of reconstruction efforts. In order to bring back the daily bustle of life in Noto and the smiles on the faces of the people there at the earliest possible timing, we will swiftly address the recovery of infrastructure. We will also advance assistance targeting the daily lives of the disaster victims and make progress in rebuilding people's livelihoods and restoring traditional industries.
8. Health and medical security
Protecting the lives and the health of the people is an important kind of security.
If we are to overcome the decline in population alongside the shrinking birthrate and aging of society, we need to have a national discussion about how we should design the benefits and burdens associated with the social security system. We will establish a national council that will include nonpartisan and expert members and discuss the comprehensive reform of social security and taxation systems, including designing a system for tax credits with benefits. This council will also include members of the opposition parties, and I hope that together we will advance our discussions on these matters.
Building upon the agreements among political parties reached thus far, we will promptly examine reviewing out-of-pocket costs for prescribed drugs similar to over-the-counter drugs, digitization at medical institutions, including electronic medical records, and the realization of efficient, high quality medical care, including through the use of digital health records to improve health care.
It is also necessary to reformulate a healthcare system that responds to the aging of society.  We will formulate a new vision for regional health care that includes not only hospitalization but also coordination with outpatient care, home-based medical care, and nursing care. We will also promote discussions within local communities. In addition, we will undertake comprehensive measures designed to rectify the uneven distribution of doctors. Alongside these efforts, we will make progress in optimizing the number of hospital beds in preparation for the new regional vision for health care.
As we press forward with these reforms to the social security system, we will keep in check the insurance premium burden shouldered by the working generation.  For issues needing immediate responses, we will expedite our discussions.
We will also work to extend healthy life expectancy by thoroughly pursuing "proactive preventive health care." We will aim to enable people to live healthy and active lives so they can contribute to the social security system. In particular, we will accelerate our responses to health issues that arise from gender differences. I have been engaged in women's lifelong health issues for many years now, and last year, the Integrated Center for Women's Health was founded. Positioning this Center as a "control tower," we will expand the outcomes all around the country by preparing facilities for medical examinations, engaging in research, and fostering human resources with regard to diseases that are unique to women.
9. Preserving our regional areas and our ways of life
(Regional future strategies)
"When discussing matters, you should start off from your own place or circumstances. Only then will steady progress be possible."
As reflected in these words by the great YOSHIDA Shoin, the vitality of local regions is in fact the vitality of Japan. This is something that I know from personal experience. We will take advantage of the growth potential of our local regions and preserve the lifestyles and the security enjoyed by the people who live there.
As one outcome of the support that the Government has provided proactively, in Kumamoto Prefecture, which Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company (TSMC) has expanded into, and in Hokkaido, where Rapidus has set up an operating base, related investments have been touched off, and various economic impacts are now beginning to emerge. Let us precipitate such instances one after another in locations all across Japan.
We will provide support for mid-sized enterprises aiming to expand their businesses beyond their local region and, by implementing bold investment promotion measures and infrastructure development in an integrated manner, we will attract large-scale investments to local areas and strategically form industrial clusters in each local region. In this way, we will promote the Strategy for the Future of Regions.
By harnessing the creation of added value through the use of technology and local resources and support for expanding businesses beyond their local areas, creating an associated population, including people who maintain residences in two areas, cultivating agriculture, forestry and fishery industry businesses that are profitable, and other endeavors, we will restore vitality to local areas, including agricultural, mountain, and fishing communities and hilly and mountainous areas.
We will also work to ensure that people can continue to live in rural areas, including young people and women. To do this, it is imperative for us to make it possible for people to receive all necessary administrative services, including high-quality education. We will work to develop local tax systems that have less disparity in revenue sources and ensure stable tax revenues.
(Policy on population; measures regarding foreign nationals in Japan)
Proceeding from the recognition that Japan's greatest problem is its shrinking population, we will create a framework for considering measures to counter the population decline, including policies on children and child-rearing.
Amidst the labor shortage that has accompanied our population decline, it is a fact that there are sectors in which foreign nationals are necessary as human resources. Inbound tourism is also important.
That said, it is also the case that a situation is now arising in which Japanese nationals are feeling unease or injustice in response to illegal activities or deviations from the rules by a subset of foreign nationals in Japan.
We draw a clear line against xenophobia. However, the government will stand firm in responding to such activities. We will enhance policy coordinating functions on foreign nationals in Japan, call for compliance with existing rules, and move forward on examining how we should set our rules governing land acquisition and other matters. To that end, I have newly appointed a minister in charge of such matters.
(Ensuring public security and safety)
We will take strict responses to new criminal activities that abuse the Internet, including by strengthening legal regulations.
In addition to steadily implementing the efforts set out in "Comprehensive Measures to Protect People from Fraud 2.0," we will accelerate necessary considerations, including regarding the legal system, and aim to eradicate so-called anonymous and mobile crime groups.
We will strengthen legal regulations in order to prevent harm from stalking or other acts that misuse new technologies and harm resulting from spousal violence.
In addition to strengthening regulations, we will conduct a review of the legal system to ensure it conforms with the times. Probation officers are very important in preventing recidivism. We will carry out measures that enhance the sustainability of the system, including enhancing measures taken to ensure probation officers' safety.  We will also move forward on discussions about reviewing the retrial system, through which finalized criminal court cases are retried.
10. Diplomacy and security
We are now witnessing historic shifts in the balance of power and an escalation of geopolitical competition, which are profoundly shaking the free, open, and stable international order that we have long embraced.
At the same time, in the area surrounding Japan, military developments and other activities of China, North Korea, and Russia -- all neighboring countries -- are a matter of grave concern.
Amid the current international situation, we will restore Japanese diplomacy that flourishes on the world's center stage.
The Japan-U.S. Alliance is the cornerstone of Japan's diplomacy and security policies. By firmly coordinating our efforts to address the challenges faced by both Japan and the United States, we will further reinforce the deterrence and the response capabilities of the Japan-U.S. Alliance. I will meet with President Trump on the occasion of his visit to Japan and work to build a relationship of trust between us, thereby elevating the Japan-U.S. Alliance to new heights.
Taking the Japan-U.S. Alliance as the cornerstone, we will also further deepen multilateral security consultations, including those among Japan, the United States, and the Republic of Korea; Japan, the United States, and the Philippines; and Japan, the United States, Australia, and India (Quad).
If we are to ensure the smooth presence of U.S. forces in Japan, gaining the understanding and cooperation of the Japanese people, including the people in the local communities directly affected, will be absolutely critical. We will continue also to work to mitigate the impact of U.S. military bases on local communities, including Okinawa Prefecture. We will press ahead with the construction work for the relocation to Henoko, aiming to realize the total return of Marine Corps Air Station (MCAS) Futenma at the earliest possible date. We will also build a robust Okinawan economy.
We will continue to vigorously advance a Free and Open Indo-Pacific as a pillar of our diplomacy and further evolve this vision in line with the times. Under this vision, we will strengthen our cooperation with like-minded countries that share fundamental values, as well as with countries in the Global South.
We will actively pursue the strategic expansion of membership in the Comprehensive and Progressive Agreement for Trans-Pacific Partnership (CPTPP).
With the Republic of Korea, an important neighboring country, we will work to further strengthen our relationship through dialogue at the summit level.
We will also further strengthen ties with ASEAN countries.
With China, an important neighboring country for Japan, it is necessary to establish a constructive and stable bilateral relationship. At the same time, it is also a fact that there are security concerns between our two countries, including those related to economic security. We will comprehensively promote a mutually beneficial relationship based on common strategic interests through continued candid dialogue between the leaders of Japan and China.
Nuclear and missile development by North Korea is totally unacceptable. As the families of the victims continue to age, the abductions issue is one of the highest priority issues for my administration. We are firmly committed to taking all necessary measures to bring all abductees home at the earliest possible time.
With regard to Russia’s aggression against Ukraine, we must not tolerate any unilateral attempts to change the status quo by force. Notwithstanding the acutely challenging circumstances enveloping Japan-Russia relations, Japan maintains its policy of resolving the territorial issue and concluding a peace treaty.
Since the formulation of the "three documents," including the National Security Strategy in December 2022, we have witnessed various changes in the security environment, such as the emergence of new ways of warfare. It is therefore essential that Japan take the initiative in fundamentally reinforcing its defense capabilities. To this end, with regard to the level of defense spending at “2 percent of GDP” as stipulated in the National Security Strategy, we will take front-loaded measures within this fiscal year, including through a supplementary budget. Furthermore, we will begin discussions with the aim of revising the "three documents" by the end of next year.
We will work to strengthen our defense production and technological bases, which constitute the core of Japan’s defense capabilities, and to improve the treatment of Self-Defense Forces personnel, who are at the center of our national defense.
11. Constitutional reform, revision of the Imperial Household Law, and commemorating the 100th anniversary of the start of the Showa era
During my tenure as prime minister, I would like to have the Diet release a proposal on constitutional reform. I very much look forward to constructive discussions that transcend party and factional lines accelerating in the Deliberative Councils on the Constitution, and I also hope that active discussions deepen among members of the public.
I also have great expectations for discussions to deepen among the various political parties and factions regarding the stable succession of the Imperial throne, leading to a revision of the Imperial Household Law.
This year would have been the 100th year of the Showa era. The year 2026 marks the centennial anniversary of the start of the Showa era. The Showa era was one in which Japan experienced unprecedented changes, including the war, the end of the war, postwar reconstruction, and rapid economic growth. Regarding this occasion as a national milestone, I wish to use related endeavors such as a commemorative ceremony and other such events to learn from the wisdom and the efforts of our predecessors, carry on their vow for peace, and make the anniversary an opportunity to lead to contributions towards the stability and prosperity of the international community.
12. Conclusion
Embracing the positions I have set forth in this speech, I will without question make Japan stronger and more prosperous and raise Japan to an elevated position in the world once again.
"One should not decide matters alone. It is necessary to discuss them appropriately with others."
In Japan, importance has been placed on the opinions of the masses since ancient times.
Politics is not about making decisions dogmatically. Instead, politics is the act of talking together, struggling together, and making decisions together.
I will engage with the members of each political party sincerely and build our future, for the sake of the nation and the Japanese people.
My fellow members of the Diet, together let us venture forward together in a new step for Japan.
Thank you for your kind attention.
Related Link

### Meeting with Families of Abductees and Others

- **Source:** Government of Japan
- **URL:** https://japan.kantei.go.jp/104/actions/202510/23menkai.html
- **Published Date:** 2025-10-23
- **Scraped Date:** 2026-03-23
- **Word Count:** 550
- **Unique ID:** `aac2cf9860030fe4`

#### Content:

Meeting with Families of Abductees and Others
October 23, 2025
Tweet
Prime Minister Takaichi holding a meeting with families of abductees and others (1)
Prime Minister Takaichi holding a meeting with families of abductees and others (2)
Prime Minister Takaichi holding a meeting with families of abductees and others (3)
Prime Minister Takaichi holding a meeting with families of abductees and others (4)
Prime Minister Takaichi holding a meeting with families of abductees and others (5)
Prime Minister Takaichi holding a meeting with families of abductees and others (6)
Prime Minister Takaichi holding a meeting with families of abductees and others (7)
Scenes from the meeting with families of abductees and others (1)
Prime Minister Takaichi holding a meeting with families of abductees and others (8)
Prime Minister Takaichi holding a meeting with families of abductees and others (9)
Scenes from the meeting with families of abductees and others (2)
[Provisional translation]
On October 23, 2025, Prime Minister Takaichi held a meeting with families of abductees and others at the Prime Minister’s Office.
The Prime Minister said in her remarks:
“Good afternoon, everyone. I am TAKAICHI Sanae, Prime Minister of Japan. Thank you very much for taking the trouble of coming all the way to the Prime Minister’s Office today. I had the opportunity to speak with Ms. YOKOTA Sakie yesterday as well, but I am happy to see you looking so well.
I would like to express my heartfelt appreciation to all the family members for engaging in various initiatives to resolve the abductions issue and lending your support to us.
The abductions issue, at its essence, is not only a violation of state sovereignty but also, as Ms. Yokota says, an issue where human lives themselves are at stake.
Twenty-three years have already passed since five abductees returned to Japan in 2002. We have not been able to realize the return of a single abductee since. I deeply regret this situation, one where family members have had no choice but to continue making heart-wrenching appeals and praying for the reunion with their loved ones.
When I think about the feelings of parents who passed away without being able to embrace their children again, I know that we must resolve the abductions issue as soon as possible, without any further delay. I sincerely take your strong wishes to heart and intend to seize every possible opportunity that will lead to the return of all the abductees to Japan at the earliest possible date.
Going forward, on the diplomatic front, I will continue to discuss the abductions issue in meetings with the leaders of various countries, including President Trump, and seek their understanding and cooperation. However, I believe that what matters most is Japan’s proactive initiatives. Building on the approaches that preceding administrations have taken toward North Korea through various channels, I will demonstrate my own leadership and work to achieve a breakthrough by any means. Needless to say, I am ready to hold a summit meeting with Chairman Kim Jong-un.
Today, I have taken this occasion to convey my strong determination to bring the abductions issue to a resolution and leverage it as a driving force to confront North Korea moving forward in my role as Prime Minister. I greatly appreciate your understanding and cooperation. Thank you very much.”

### Press Conference by Prime Minister TAKAICHI Sanae regarding Her Aspirations upon the Inauguration of Her New Cabinet, the Upcoming Diplomatic Schedule, and Other Matters

- **Source:** Government of Japan
- **URL:** https://japan.kantei.go.jp/104/statement/202510/22bura2.html
- **Published Date:** 2025-10-22
- **Scraped Date:** 2026-03-23
- **Word Count:** 308
- **Unique ID:** `3ec2a666ff16462e`

#### Content:

Press Conference by Prime Minister TAKAICHI Sanae regarding Her Aspirations upon the Inauguration of Her New Cabinet, the Upcoming Diplomatic Schedule, and Other Matters
October 22, 2025
Tweet
[Provisional translation]
(On Prime Minister Takaichi's aspirations as her cabinet officially begins its duties; on her upcoming trips to attend the ASEAN and APEC summits; and the stance she intends to take during her upcoming meeting with U.S. President Trump)
First of all, as I mentioned earlier, a missile was launched from North Korea this morning. The Takaichi Cabinet views crisis management regarding this matter as absolutely critical in protecting people's lives. We intend to maintain an even higher level of vigilance than in the past and respond swiftly.
Also, yesterday, I was designated as prime minister at the Diet in the afternoon, and then in the evening I was invested with the office by His Majesty the Emperor, thereby assuming the post of prime minister of Japan. I call my cabinet the Cabinet of Determination and Progress. Once we have jointly taken a well-considered decision on something, we will fully commit ourselves to moving forward on it, stepping up the pace as we do so. Everything we do will be for the sake of Japan and the Japanese people.
In addition, my diplomatic schedule will get off to an early start. First will be the ASEAN summit, and then President Trump is scheduled to visit Japan. The APEC summit follows soon afterward. Each of those diplomatic occasions is of tremendous importance to Japan.
To begin with, in order to restore Japanese diplomacy that flourishes on the world's center stage, I will work to deepen my friendships with various people, most notably with other national leaders, and forge relationships of trust with them. Beyond that, going forward, I intend to communicate clearly what Japan aims to achieve as a nation.

---

### Press Conference by Prime Minister Takaichi regarding the Ballistic Missile Launch by North Korea

- **Source:** Government of Japan
- **URL:** https://japan.kantei.go.jp/104/statement/202510/22bura1.html
- **Published Date:** 2025-10-22
- **Scraped Date:** 2026-03-23
- **Word Count:** 156
- **Unique ID:** `8d243df73f87180a`

#### Content:

Press Conference by Prime Minister Takaichi regarding the Ballistic Missile Launch by North Korea
October 22, 2025
Tweet
[Provisional translation]
Good morning.
Receiving the announcement from the Republic of Korea that North Korea had launched a ballistic missile, I changed my outing schedule and have just been briefed on the situation.
We are taking all possible measures in close coordination among Japan, the United States and the Republic of Korea, including through real-time sharing of missile warning data. The Japanese Government is fully aware of this latest launch. Based on what we have just confirmed, no missile has flown into Japan’s territorial waters or Exclusive Economic Zone (EEZ). Furthermore, no reports of damage have been confirmed by relevant agencies.
Nevertheless, to ensure the safety of the people, the Takaichi Cabinet has just instructed the Minister of Defense and the Minister of Foreign Affairs to continue to collect and analyze the necessary information. That’s all from me.
